definition: Clinging to doctrines, narratives, or theories of self as something to hold, defend, or identify with.
notes: The project keeps clinging to self-doctrine as the default because it names the doctrinal function directly and preserves the relation to view and self-making. This term should be read closely with sakkāyadiṭṭhi, anattā, and upādāna. It belongs to the four clinging types and points to appropriation of self-accounts, not merely abstract philosophy.

context_rules.notes: Allowed alternate when brevity is needed.

context_rules.context: In explanatory prose emphasizing appropriative holding of self-theories
context_rules.notes: Use when the practical movement of upādāna should be made more explicit.

example_phrases.notes: One member of the fourfold set.

version_notes: Promoted from a stub to a major entry and tied to not-self, identity-view, and clinging analysis.
